does: This paper proposes a comprehensive framework for collecting preference data in RLHF systems, decomposing the process into four sequential steps: prompt generation, response generation, response filtering, and human labeling. The work systematically investigates how preference data should be collected to train reward models effectively, questioning whether standard pairwise comparison approaches adequately filter noise and maintain diversity. Through comprehensive experiments, the authors demonstrate how each component of their framework impacts the quality and efficiency of preference data collection, with particular attention to reducing human labeling burden while maintaining data quality for downstream reward model training.
connects: This work builds on the growing RLHF literature that uses reward models as a central component for aligning LLMs with human preferences. It extends prior research that largely treated preference data collection as a black box, responding to recent studies showing variation in how preference data is sourced from either AI systems or human raters. The paper connects to broader work on data quality, diversity, and annotation efficiency in machine learning, particularly building on existing frameworks for understanding how data collection pipeline design affects model performance. It also relates to concurrent efforts in the community to scrutinize and improve each stage of RLHF, moving beyond treating preference annotation as a simple binary labeling task.
enables: This framework enables future work to systematically optimize each stage of preference data collection rather than treating it as a monolithic process. It opens pathways for developing more efficient human annotation strategies by identifying where computational filtering can reduce labeling costs without sacrificing quality. The decomposed approach facilitates research into automated response filtering techniques and prompt generation strategies that improve data diversity. This work also enables better practices for constructing preference datasets at scale, potentially improving reward model training and downstream LLM alignment. Future research can build on these insights to develop adaptive data collection strategies that dynamically adjust sampling based on model performance signals.
